Package org.apache.juneau.commons.logging

Class LogRecord

java.lang.Object
java.util.logging.LogRecord
org.apache.juneau.commons.logging.LogRecord
All Implemented Interfaces:
Serializable
public class LogRecord extends LogRecord
LogRecord that supports lazy message formatting.

This class extends java.util.logging.LogRecord and overrides getMessage() to use StringUtils.format(String, Object...) for lazy formatting. The message is only formatted when getMessage() is actually called.

Usage:

This class is used internally by Logger when logging formatted messages. The formatted message is only computed when the LogRecord's message is actually accessed (e.g., by a Handler or Formatter).

See Also:
  • Logger
  • StringUtils.format(String, Object...)
  • LogRecord

  • Constructor Details

    • LogRecord

      public LogRecord(String loggerName, Level level, String msg, Object[] parameters, Throwable throwable)
      Constructor.
      Parameters:
      loggerName - The logger name.
      level - The log level.
      msg - The message pattern (will be used as-is if args is null or empty).
      parameters - The format arguments, or null if none.
      throwable - The throwable, or null if none.

  • Method Details

    • getMessage

      public String getMessage()
      Returns the log message, formatting it lazily if needed.

      If parameters were set via LogRecord.setParameters(Object[]), the message is formatted using StringUtils.format(String, Object...) on first access.

      Overrides:
      getMessage in class LogRecord
      Returns:
      The log message, formatted if parameters are present.

    • getSourceClassName

      public String getSourceClassName()
      Returns the source class name, calculating it lazily from the stack trace if not already set.
      Overrides:
      getSourceClassName in class LogRecord
      Returns:
      The source class name, or null if not available.

    • getSourceMethodName

      public String getSourceMethodName()
      Returns the source method name, calculating it lazily from the stack trace if not already set.
      Overrides:
      getSourceMethodName in class LogRecord
      Returns:
      The source method name, or null if not available.

    • formatted

      public String formatted(String format)
      Formats this log record as a string using the specified format pattern.

      Similar to how SimpleFormatter formats log records, this method allows you to specify a custom format string with placeholders that are replaced with actual values from the log record.

      The format string supports both named placeholders (e.g., "{date}") and Formatter-style format specifiers (e.g., "%1$tc"). Named placeholders are replaced first, then the resulting string is formatted using Formatter with the following arguments:

      1. date - The date/time as a Date object
      2. source - The source class name and method name (e.g., "com.example.MyClass myMethod")
      3. logger - The logger name
      4. level - The log level
      5. message - The formatted log message
      6. thrown - The throwable object (if any)
      Named Placeholders:
      • "{date}" - The date/time formatted using SimpleFormatter's default date format
      • "{timestamp}" - The date/time formatted as ISO-8601 (yyyy-MM-dd'T'HH:mm:ss.SSSZ)
      • "{class}" - The source class name
      • "{method}" - The source method name
      • "{source}" - The source class name and method name (e.g., "com.example.MyClass myMethod")
      • "{logger}" - The logger name
      • "{level}" - The log level name
      • "{msg}" - The log message (formatted if parameters are present)
      • "{thread}" - The thread ID
      • "{threadid}" - The thread ID (alias for {thread})
      • "{exception}" - The exception message (if thrown)
      • "{thrown}" - The throwable object (if thrown)
      Formatter-Style Format Specifiers:

      You can use Formatter-style format specifiers directly in the format string. The arguments are passed in the same order as SimpleFormatter:

      • "%1$s" or "%1$tc" - The date/time (argument 1)
      • "%2$s" - The source (argument 2)
      • "%3$s" - The logger name (argument 3)
      • "%4$s" - The log level (argument 4)
      • "%5$s" - The log message (argument 5)
      • "%6$s" - The throwable and its backtrace (argument 6)

      For date/time formatting, you can use date/time conversion characters with argument 1:

      • "%1$tc" - Complete date and time (e.g., "Tue Mar 22 13:11:31 PDT 2011")
      • "%1$tb" - Month abbreviation (e.g., "Mar")
      • "%1$td" - Day of month (01-31)
      • "%1$tY" - Year (4 digits)
      • "%1$tH" - Hour (00-23)
      • "%1$tM" - Minute (00-59)
      • "%1$tS" - Second (00-59)
      • "%1$tL" - Milliseconds (000-999)
      • "%1$tN" - Nanoseconds (000000000-999999999, since JDK 9)
      • "%1$Tp" - AM/PM marker (uppercase)
      • "%1$tp" - AM/PM marker (lowercase)

      The format string can also contain "%n" for newlines.

      Examples:

      LogRecord record = new LogRecord(Level.INFO, "User {0} logged in", "John"); // Using named placeholders String formatted1 = record.formatted("[{timestamp} {level}] {msg}"); // Result: "[2025-01-22T07:42:45.123-0500 INFO] User John logged in" // Using Formatter-style specifiers String formatted2 = record.formatted("%4$s: %5$s [%1$tc]%n"); // Result: "INFO: User John logged in [Tue Jan 22 07:42:45 PST 2025]" // Mixed named placeholders and Formatter specifiers String formatted3 = record.formatted("%1$tb %1$td, %1$tY {level}: {msg}%n"); // Result: "Jan 22, 2025 INFO: User John logged in"

      Parameters:
      format - The format string with placeholders and/or Formatter-style format specifiers.
      Returns:
      The formatted string.
      See Also:
      • SimpleFormatter
      • Formatter
